finding: Boundary is well-scoped as a design artifact but conflates 3 surfaces (CLI, IPC, headless) into one design without separation. The CLI entry point (`opencode doctor`) doesn't exist in the desktop package — there is no `bin` entry in `packages/desktop/package.json`, no yargs/commander dependency, and no CLI entry point. The existing CLI lives in `packages/opencode/package.json` (a separate package). The doctor should define its core engine first, then surface-specific adapters.

DD-04 (PGlite) and DD-10 (MCP Servers) are designed as main-process checks but PGlite and MCP run in the sidecar process (utilityProcess.fork). The main process cannot directly check PGlite connectivity — it must communicate via the sidecar's HTTP API or IPC message bus. The plan doesn't design this cross-process bridge. Without it, DD-04 and DD-10 will fail at runtime because PGlite lives in packages/opencode/src/storage/db.pg.ts (sidecar process), not packages/desktop/.

The plan uses plain async/await with Promise.allSettled for the doctor service, but the existing diagnostic infrastructure (Flight Recorder at packages/opencode/src/debug/flight-recorder.ts, Debug Packet at debug-packet.ts, sidecar lifecycle at packages/desktop/src/main/index.ts) uses Effect-TS (Service, Ref, Layer, Effect.gen, Effect.timeout). This creates a two-tier architecture where diagnostic services speak different languages. The doctor should be an Effect service registered in the same dependency graph for consistency and reuse.

DD-04 (PGlite) could cause database corruption if the main process attempts to initialize a PGlite connection while the sidecar process already has one open. PGlite uses WASM-based embedded Postgres with single-process assumptions. DD-05 (DuckDB) bypasses the connection pool (max 4 connections) by spawning an independent CLI subprocess, potentially exhausting DB resources. DD-12 (Auto-Update) could trigger unintended downloads — the plan doesn't specify using check-for-update mode vs. download mode. DD-07 (Config) proposes a write test but doesn't specify using a test key with atomic write-then-delete.

19 new files + 6 modified IPC/preload files is large for a single feature. 14 check files for individual checks is over-split — many checks (DD-07 Config, DD-12 Updater, DD-13 Windows, DD-14 Network) are 1-5 line probes that don't merit their own file. The DoctorReport type becomes part of the IPC contract surface, meaning any schema change requires updating ipc-contract.ts, preload/types.ts, AND the renderer. Consider a flat CheckFn array in constants.ts rather than N files.

The runner uses Promise.allSettled with all 14 checks immediately — no concurrency limit. On a low-resource machine, 14 parallel checks doing native module loading (DD-03), PGlite WASM init (DD-04), DuckDB CLI spawn (DD-05), HTTP requests (DD-14), and filesystem probes simultaneously could cause OOM or resource starvation. DD-03 (native module loading) can crash the Electron process on unsupported platforms if require() fails catastrophically — the plan doesn't mention process-level crash guards. DD-02 (Gatekeeper) only works on macOS; DD-14 (Network) depends on external service availability which will always fail in CI/offline. Platform guards and pre-flight checks are missing.

REVISED: Investigation revealed that packages/desktop/src/main/ has zero Effect Service/Layer usage — all 9 service modules (server.ts, updater.ts, logging.ts, sidecar.ts, etc.) are plain async/await. Effect is used only in index.ts as an entry point orchestration wrapper via Effect.promise(() => asyncFn()). The doctor using async/await with Promise.allSettled follows established convention. The initial Effect-TS concern was based on a mistaken assumption about the codebase's architecture.
